content:
Continuing from the last Morning Light, we saw that the coverings of the tabernacle were not only beautiful and meaningful, but they also had to travel. When the cloud moved, the tabernacle moved. And when the tabernacle moved, the coverings were not left behind. They had to be carried forward.
This teaches a very practical truth: God’s work is not only something we admire; it is something we must bear. Carrying is not only transportation. Carrying means responsibility. Carrying means keeping what God has given. Carrying means preserving it through the wilderness journey of life.

Spiritually, carrying represents holding what God has placed into our hands.
It means living out what we have received. It means guarding His word,His truth, and His ways through our daily choices. It means keeping our covering; Christ Himself - throughout our whole life. It is easy to begin well and then drop what God gave. It is easy to be moved by truth and then grow careless. But the tabernacle coverings show us that the holy things must be carried day after day. The journey does not pause because we feel tired.The cloud moves, and we must move with it.

Paul shows this spirit of carrying. He said, “I bear on my body the marks of the Lord Jesus” (Galatians 6:17). Paul did not carry his faith only in his mouth.

He carried it in his body. His scars were like a record that he belonged to Christ. He carried this through his whole life. This is the kind of carrying that the New Creation life calls us into. Not only a belief we speak, but a devotion we bear.

Now God did not tell the Gershonites to carry these coverings without help. God gave them support. He gave them two wagons. And this is where the picture becomes very personal. A wagon is something that carries weight for us. It helps move what we cannot carry alone. It makes the load possible. In the same way, God has given His people support systems to carry spiritual responsibility. When the coverings are heavy, when the journey is long, when endurance is required, God does not leave His people helpless.He provides help so the burden can be carried faithfully.

So what can the two wagons teach us spiritually? The first wagon can be understood as the Word of God. The Word gives direction, strength, and clarity. Psalm 119:105 says, “Your word is a lamp to guide my feet and a light for my path.” The Word helps us carry life correctly. It shows where to step and where not to step. It exposes danger. It corrects wrong thinking. It keeps us from drifting. Many people fall not because they wanted to fall, but because they walked without light. The Word is God’s wagon for the journey. It carries our mind when the mind feels weak. It carries our conscience when the heart wants to excuse sin. It carries our decisions when confusion rises. Without the Word, the load becomes too heavy.

The second wagon can be understood as the Spirit of God that strengthens us in the journey. God does not only speak commands; He also gives strength to obey. When the flesh is tired and the mind is weak, God’s Spirit gives help. Scripture teaches that believers are to walk by the Spirit so they do not fulfill the desires of the flesh (Galatians 5:16). This is not only a rule; it is a provision. The Spirit strengthens the inner man. The Spirit reminds, convicts, comforts, and guides. The Word shows the way, and the Spirit gives strength to walk that way. Together, these two wagons carry the coverings through the wilderness.

And why not one wagon? Why two? God loves confirmation and balance. Scripture teaches that a matter is established by two witnesses. Two strengthens certainty. Two removes doubt. Two provides support. Ecclesiastes 4:9 says, “Two people are better off than one, for they can help each other succeed.”

The journey with God is not meant to be carried by one weak support.God gives more than one help. God gives balance. God gives confirmation.God gives what is needed to succeed. The Word and the Spirit agree. The Word gives truth. The Spirit makes that truth living inside. The Word corrects.The Spirit strengthens. The Word speaks. The Spirit empowers. This is how God helps us carry the holy coverings through life.

But wagons do not move by themselves. A wagon can be full and still sit still if nothing pulls it. That is why God also gave oxen. The text says God gave four oxen along with the two wagons. Oxen are known for pulling heavy loads, doing hard labor, and enduring long work. They are not quick like horses. They are steady, patient, and strong. They keep pulling even when the path is dusty and the day is long. Spiritually, oxen represent strength to serve God faithfully, endurance in carrying God’s work, and labor in obedience.

Scripture even uses oxen as a picture of workers. Paul quotes the law, “You shall not muzzle an ox while it treads out the grain” (1 Corinthians 9:9). This shows that oxen are workers who labor continuously. They keep moving. They keep serving. They do not stop because the work is repetitive. They do not stop because the load is heavy. They keep treading until the grain is separated. In the same way, God’s servants are called to steady labor. The New Creation life is not only moments of strong emotion. It is daily obedience, daily discipline, daily endurance.

Oxen also teach us submission. Oxen do not choose their own path. They do not decide their own work. They submit and pull. They follow the direction given. In the same way, we are called to submit our will to God. Many people want to carry holy things while still steering their own life. But oxen do not lead; they follow and pull. This is a very sharp lesson. If we want to carry Christ’s covering through life, our will must come under God. The strength is not only muscle strength. It is surrendered strength.

Proverbs 14:4 gives another insight. It says that without oxen the manger is clean, but much increase comes by the strength of the ox. In simple words, oxen bring harvest, but they also bring mess, effort, and daily work. People who want a clean manger often do not want oxen. They want comfort without labor. But if there is to be a harvest, oxen are needed. Spiritually, if there is to be fruit, endurance and labor are needed. And when Christ said the harvest is great and the laborers are few, the heart understands the same principle: the work is heavy, and true workers are not many. A true worker is like an ox; steady, obedient, faithful, not choosing his own path, but pulling under God’s direction.

So now we can see the picture clearly. The coverings represent our life of being covered by Christ, separated to God, and protected under His care. Carrying those coverings means responsibility through the journey. Two wagons show God’s support; His Word and His Spirit, to help us carry. Oxen show the strength and endurance of obedient workers who submit their will and keep pulling faithfully.

But one question remains. Why four oxen? God could have given two.
God could have given six. He gave four. There is meaning here that we should not rush past. We will study the meaning of the four oxen in our upcoming Morning Light.

title: Prayer
text:
Yahweh and God our Father, thank You for not leaving us alone in the journey.
Thank You for giving us Your Word as light and Your Spirit as strength to carry what is holy.
Make us faithful workers like oxen; steady, obedient, patient, and willing to labor for Your purpose.
Help us submit our will to You, so we do not choose our own path but follow Your direction.
Strengthen us to carry the covering of Christ through every season, until our race is finished.
Keep us from dropping what You have given, and let our life remain set apart for You.
In Christ name, amen.
